scenario: User picks 'all', 'docs', 'code' before searching

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Dropdown click is friction, scope not visible mid-search, no keyboard

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Tabs feel like filters not scopes, scope lost on URL refresh

v3_senior.code:
// Inline scope prefix: 'docs: react query' parses scope from query
const {scope,terms}=parseQuery(query);// Auto-suggest scope as user types 'doc'
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Power-user friendly, URL-syncable as query, scope is part of search not separate UI

v4_staff.code:
// Algolia/Meilisearch: federated search, score across scopes, UI groups results by scope automatically
const {hits}=useInfiniteHits();// hits grouped by source
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Single search, multi-scope results ranked together, server-side grouping

v5_principal.code:
// Search as semantic intent. User types 'how do I auth users' — system routes to docs (not code), uses LLM rerank. Hybrid: BM25 + vector + reranker.
// Telemetry: track scope confusion, optimize over time.
v5_principal.the_realization: Scopes are friction. Better: infer intent from query. 'docs:' for explicit, semantic routing for implicit. Search products are converging on intent inference; explicit scopes feel old-school.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for power users, v4 for SaaS, v5 for AI-era products

scenario: User uploads large file, closes tab; upload continues

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Tab close kills upload

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: User must allow SW, SW can be terminated by browser, no Background Fetch API

v3_senior.code:
// Background Fetch API (Chrome)
const bgFetch=await registration.backgroundFetch.fetch('upload-1',['/up'],{title:'Uploading photo',icons,downloadTotal:file.size});bgFetch.addEventListener('progress',()=>updateUI(bgFetch))
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Browser-managed, survives tab close, native notification, progress in OS

v4_staff.code:
// Fallback chain: Background Fetch → service worker upload queue → tus.io resume on next visit
// Detect support, choose best available
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Progressive enhancement, graceful fallback, resume protocol means worst case isn't loss

v5_principal.code:
// File transfer as platform feature. PWA + share target + Background Fetch + tus resume = Dropbox-like reliability on web. Notify on completion via Push.
// Most apps don't need this — but media apps (video editor, Figma) do.
v5_principal.the_realization: Background uploads were impossible on web until recently. Now they're table stakes for media apps. Most engineers don't know Background Fetch exists. Knowing what the platform offers is half the senior work.

when_to_use_which_version: v3-v5 only for media-heavy apps

scenario: Mixed aspect ratios; tight packing without distortion

v1_junior.code:
// CSS grid with fixed cells, images object-cover
.grid{grid-template-columns:repeat(auto-fill,200px)}img{height:200px;object-fit:cover}
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Crops photos, no aspect preservation, wasteful gaps

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Vertical order not visual order (reading top→bottom not left→right), lazy loading order off

v3_senior.code:
// Justified layout (Flickr-style)
import {layout} from 'justified-layout';const result=layout(photos.map(p=>({width:p.w,height:p.h})),{targetRowHeight:200,boxSpacing:8})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Justified rows, no cropping, predictable reading order

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Native where supported, polyfill where not, accessible (semantic order)

v5_principal.code:
// Layout is product. Apple Photos uses ML for grouping (related photos same size). Pinterest uses masonry. Instagram uses square crop. The layout is part of the brand.
// Pre-compute on server, send positions to client.
v5_principal.the_realization: Photo grids are layout puzzles. Crop = simple but ugly; masonry = elegant but order-confusing; justified = best general. ML-aware (highlight best photo larger) is the frontier. Default crop = giving up.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for galleries, v4 progressively, v5 for media apps

scenario: Resizable side-by-side panels (IDE-like)

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Cursor doesn't change globally, selection happens, no touch, no bounds

v2_mid_level.code: // On mousedown: setIsDragging, add document mousemove/mouseup. document.body.style.cursor='col-resize';userSelect='none'
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No min/max constraints, no double-click reset, no keyboard, no persistence

v3_senior.code:
// Pointer events for touch/mouse, bounds, persistence to localStorage, keyboard (arrow keys when handle focused)
<div role='separator' aria-orientation='vertical' aria-valuenow={width} tabIndex={0} onKeyDown={e=>{if(e.key==='ArrowLeft')setWidth(w-10)}}>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Accessible (separator role), keyboard, touch, persistence

v4_staff.code:
// react-resizable-panels — purpose-built, recursive splits, collapsible, keyboard
<PanelGroup direction='horizontal'><Panel defaultSize={30} collapsible/><PanelResizeHandle/><Panel/></PanelGroup>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Composable nested splits, save/restore layouts, collapsible, robust

v5_principal.code:
// Layouts are product. Named workspaces (debug, write, review), per-user saved layouts, share layout URLs. Treat layout as document.
// VS Code workbench is the gold standard.
v5_principal.the_realization: Resizable panels are the start of workspace customization. Linear's three-pane, VS Code's everywhere-pane, Figma's panels — these are products built on resizable layouts with persistence and presets. One-off splitters miss the value.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 always, v5 for IDEs/tools

scenario: Long operation (export, upload) — show toast with progress

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Two toasts, no progress, blocks UI, no cancel

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No progress percentage, no cancel button

v3_senior.code: const id=toast.custom(<ProgressToast progress={0}/>,{duration:Infinity});const ctrl=new AbortController();export({signal:ctrl.signal,onProgress:p=>toast.custom(<ProgressToast progress={p}/>,{id})}).then(()=>toast.success('Done',{id}))
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Live progress, cancel button, updates in place

v4_staff.code:
// Persistent task model: tasks live beyond toast. Toast is one view; task center is another. Task survives navigation.
const {task}=useTask(taskId);// task.progress, task.cancel, task.status
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Task is first-class entity, can navigate while running, view in task center, multiple tasks supported

v5_principal.code:
// Long tasks are background jobs. Server-side queue, frontend subscribes. Resilient to refresh. Notifications when done (push). Same model serves UI toast + email digest.
// Background Tasks API (proposed) for client-side.
v5_principal.the_realization: Long task UX is hardest when treated as 'a loading state'. Treated as 'a job', it's: queue, persistence, multiple views (toast/center/email), survival across reload, cancellation. Junior writes a spinner; senior architects a job system.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for medium-long, v4-v5 for serious products

scenario: User scans QR code, enters 6-digit code to enable 2FA

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No backup codes, no fallback, lockout risk, screen reader can't read QR

v2_mid_level.code:
// Show secret as text fallback for screen readers / no-camera ;
Manual: {secret}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No backup codes still, 6-digit input UX poor, no recovery

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Native one-time-code autocomplete (iOS reads SMS), backup codes mandatory, recovery flow

v4_staff.code:
// WebAuthn/Passkeys as primary, TOTP as fallback. Passkey is more secure, more convenient.
const cred=await navigator.credentials.create({publicKey:{...}});// User uses Face ID/Touch ID
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Passkey-first reduces lockout, no shared secret, phishing-proof

v5_principal.code:
// Authentication choice = adversary choice. Threat model: phishing? → passkey. Device theft? → biometric step-up. Account recovery is the real attack surface — design it as if attacker is using it.
// Recovery: identity verification (gov ID, video call), waiting period.
v5_principal.the_realization: 2FA is one mitigation in a portfolio. Designing for the recovery flow is harder than the enrollment flow — and that's where takeovers happen. Junior focuses on the cool QR code; senior focuses on what happens when the phone is lost.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — QR + input, no backup codes
when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for new apps, v5 for high-value

scenario: Respect prefers-reduced-motion for animations

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Vestibular discomfort, accessibility violation

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: JS animations not covered (Framer Motion), all-or-nothing

v3_senior.code:
// Framer Motion respects automatically; for custom JS:
const reduce=useReducedMotion();<motion.div animate={reduce?{}:{x:100}}/>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: JS animation respects preference, sensible alternatives (fade instead of slide)

v4_staff.code:
// Selective reduction: reduce vestibular triggers (parallax, big movement, autoplay video) but keep meaningful ones (focus state).
// 'reduce' ≠ 'none' — keep transitions, kill motion.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Nuanced — opacity transitions OK, transform movements not. Knows what triggers vestibular issues.

v5_principal.code:
// Motion as design system axis. Tokens: --motion-duration-fast, --motion-easing-out. Reduce-motion variant changes tokens, components inherit. CI checks no hardcoded durations.
// User can also disable autoplay, hover effects, hover triggers.
v5_principal.the_realization: Reduced motion isn't 'turn off animations' — it's 'replace vestibular triggers with non-vestibular alternatives'. Token system makes this scale. Hard-coding durations means manual rewrite for every component.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for design systems

scenario: Prefetch route data when user hovers link

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No prefetch — click waits for everything

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Wasted on items user doesn't click, mobile has no hover, prefetches even slow networks

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Smart prefetch (connection-aware, visibility-aware, intent-aware)

v4_staff.code:
// Next.js / TanStack Router handle this automatically with viewport detection. Use built-in.
<Link prefetch={true} href='/items/123'>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Framework handles intelligently, RSC prefetch streams data + code, automatic

v5_principal.the_realization: Speculation Rules let the browser pre-render full pages. Instant navigation. Most engineers don't know it exists. The platform keeps adding capabilities that obsolete hand-rolled solutions.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 if no framework, v4 with framework, v5 for cutting edge

scenario: Where to put React error boundaries

v1_junior.code: // One at top, wraps whole app
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Any error blanks whole app, no recovery, lose all state

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Boundaries scattered ad hoc, no reset, no error reporting

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Hierarchy of boundaries, reset on key change, telemetry, sensible fallbacks per level

v4_staff.code:
// Suspense + ErrorBoundary as paired primitives. Loading and error are both boundary-driven.
// React Router / Next.js loaders surface errors to nearest boundary automatically.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Suspense + Error as composition primitives, framework-integrated, predictable

v5_principal.code:
// Error boundaries are part of resilience design. Combined with: graceful degradation (chart fails → table view), bug reporting UI (in-context), session replay (LogRocket).
// User experience of failure is a designed surface.
v5_principal.the_realization: Error boundaries aren't a try-catch — they're resilience architecture. Where to place them is design decision: feature-level for graceful degradation, widget-level for isolation. Wrapping the app teaches nothing; boundary-everywhere is noise.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 with framework, v5 for serious products

scenario: Find expensive component renders

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Dev only, manual, samples not representative

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Production data missing, no aggregation, no flame graph

v3_senior.code:
// Profiler in production with sampling. Send aggregated to backend.
onRender:(id,phase,dur)=>{if(dur>16&&Math.random()<0.01)send({id,phase,dur})}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Production sampling, threshold-based capture, aggregation

v4_staff.code:
// React Compiler eliminates most manual memoization. Profile shows components Compiler couldn't optimize.
// Pair with LoAF for actual user impact.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Trust Compiler for memo, profile what's left, correlate with LoAF for impact

v5_principal.code:
// Performance work is bisection: which deploy regressed? Profile-driven regressions via CI. Lighthouse CI catches LCP/INP. Component renders aren't the bottleneck for most apps — bundle/network/server is.
// Profile only after measuring impact.
v5_principal.the_realization: Render performance is the last place to optimize, not the first. Bundle size, network waterfalls, server latency dominate. Premature render optimization with useMemo everywhere is technical debt. React Compiler is releasing engineers from this.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for serious apps, v4 with React Compiler, v5 always measure first

scenario: Access previous value of state/prop

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Subtle render-vs-effect timing, undefined first render, no comparison helpers

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Always one render behind, doesn't notify of change

v3_senior.code:
// Question why you need it. Usually: derive state, useEffect with dependency, or move to event handler
// Anti-pattern: 'compare prev and current in render' — use useEffect dependency
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Recognizes usePrevious as smell, prefers derived state and effect dependencies

v4_staff.code:
// When genuinely needed: use during render compute via useState/useRef pattern from React docs
function usePrevious(val){const [tuple,set]=useState([null,val]);if(tuple[1]!==val)set([tuple[1],val]);return tuple[0]}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Updates during render (one render less behind), works in concurrent React

v5_principal.code:
// 99% of usePrevious usage indicates state design problem. State should derive from current values, or be event-driven. 'I need previous to compare' = 'I should reduce on events'.
// useReducer makes this explicit.
v5_principal.the_realization: usePrevious is a code smell more than a hook. Wanting previous values usually means imperative thinking in declarative React. Refactor to event-driven (reducer) or derived (selector) — previous disappears.

when_to_use_which_version: avoid; refactor to v5 thinking

scenario: Show/hide fields based on other field values

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Hidden field still in form data, validation runs, no animation

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Schema validation still requires it, conditional schemas hard

v3_senior.code:
// Conditional Zod schema
const schema=z.discriminatedUnion('type',[z.object({type:z.literal('person'),name:z.string()}),z.object({type:z.literal('company'),name:z.string(),taxId:z.string()})])
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Discriminated union makes conditional fields type-safe, validation correct per branch

v4_staff.code:
// Conditional + animated reveal + focus management
<AnimatePresence>{type==='company'&&<motion.div initial={{height:0}} animate={{height:'auto'}}><Input autoFocus name='taxId'/></motion.div>}</AnimatePresence>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Smooth reveal, focus moves to new field (one-handed mobile), no layout shift glitch

v5_principal.code:
// Form is a tree of conditional branches. Use form schema language (JSON Schema, GraphQL input types). UI renders from schema. Backend validates from same schema.
// SurveyJS / Tripetto for complex flows.
v5_principal.the_realization: Conditional forms are decision trees. Hardcoding 'if type === company' is the wrong primitive. Schema-driven forms separate logic from rendering, allow A/B testing flow without code changes.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — ships, ghost data in form
when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for polish, v5 for many forms

scenario: Show placeholder shapes while loading

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Spinner gives no info, jarring transition, no progressive reveal

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Generic skeleton doesn't match content, animation drains battery

v3_senior.code:
// Skeleton matches actual layout (same heights, shapes). Stop animation when loaded.
<SkeletonList rows={5}/>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Content-aware skeleton (reduces layout shift), removes when content arrives, prefers-reduced-motion respected

v4_staff.code:
// Suspense + ErrorBoundary + Skeleton fallback. Loading is structural, not stateful.
<Suspense fallback={<SkeletonList/>}><AsyncList/></Suspense>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Suspense as composition, loading state declarative, works with RSC streaming

v5_principal.code:
// Question whether skeleton is right. Often: SSR/RSC means no loading state. Optimistic UI means immediate result. Skeleton is for inherent latency, not for poor architecture.
// Stale-while-revalidate: show last data, refresh in background — no skeleton.
v5_principal.the_realization: Skeletons are admission of latency. Better: eliminate the latency (RSC, prefetch, cache). When unavoidable, content-aware skeleton minimizes shift. Generic gray boxes are giving up.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 with Suspense, v5 eliminate need

scenario: What closes a modal? Backdrop, escape, button, route change?

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Esc doesn't close (a11y fail), backdrop click ambiguous, no auto-close on route change

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Backdrop click — should it close? Depends on form dirty state. No global stack management.

v3_senior.code:
// Dirty-aware close: prompt if unsaved changes
function handleClose(){if(dirty&&!confirm('Discard changes?'))return;onClose()};// All exits (esc, backdrop, button) go through same function
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Single close path with dirty check, accessible, route change also triggers

v4_staff.code:
// Use Radix/Headless UI Dialog — handles esc, backdrop config (dismissible: true/false), focus return, scroll lock
<Dialog onOpenChange={(open)=>!open&&handleClose()}>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles all close paths uniformly, dismissibility configurable, accessibility built-in

v5_principal.code:
// Dismissibility is a property of the action. Confirm dialogs = non-dismissible. Detail views = dismissible. Forms with data = dirty-check. Codify in design system.
<Dialog dismissible='unless-dirty'>
v5_principal.the_realization: Modal close paths reveal whether you've thought about user state. 'Click backdrop to close' destroys data; 'always confirm' is friction. The pattern is: cheap actions dismiss freely, expensive actions confirm, dirty data prompts.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — esc works, dirty data destroyed
when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 use library, v5 codify in DS

scenario: User clicks 'export'; download all data as CSV

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Commas in values break, no headers, no escaping, encoding issues, only current page

v2_mid_level.code:
// Proper CSV: papaparse
import Papa from 'papaparse';const csv=Papa.unparse(data);// Handles quotes, escapes, headers
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Only client data (filtered/paginated), no progress for large export, Excel UTF-8 BOM

v3_senior.code:
// Server-side export: streaming CSV from API
const res=await fetch('/export.csv?'+filters);const blob=await res.blob();download(blob);// Add UTF-8 BOM for Excel: '\ufeff'+csv
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: All data not just visible, server-side filtering applied, Excel-compatible UTF-8

v4_staff.code:
// Async export with job: POST → returns jobId, poll/SSE for progress, download when ready
const job=await api.startExport({filters});const blob=await pollUntilReady(job.id)
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Doesn't block UI for huge exports, progress shown, email-when-ready option

v5_principal.code:
// Export is a feature. Multiple formats (CSV, Excel, PDF), customizable columns, scheduled exports (weekly to S3), API access. Treat as data integration not button.
// Pair with Reports API.
v5_principal.the_realization: Export starts as button, ends as data integration: scheduled, filtered, formatted, delivered to integrations. Engineers ship CSV download and discover users want webhook → BI tool.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for big data, v5 for B2B

scenario: Show recent queries when input focused

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No dedup, no LRU, no clear, no per-user (shared device leak)

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No clear UI, no per-account when logged in, exposes history to other accounts

v3_senior.code:
// Key by userId, clear option, recent rendered above suggestions
const key=`recent:${userId}`;// + Clear all + Remove individual
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Per-user, manageable, integrated with suggestions UI

v4_staff.code: // Server-side recent: searched across devices. Privacy-respecting (deletable). Combine with frequently-searched (population data) for new users.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Cross-device, recoverable, includes social proof (popular searches)

v5_principal.code:
// Recent + frequent + suggested. ML: 'searches like yours'. Privacy controls. GDPR right-to-delete.
// Track click-through: recent shown but not clicked = remove faster.
v5_principal.the_realization: Search history is product (engagement, conversion) and privacy (regulation, trust). Half-implementing it leaks data across user accounts; full-implementing it is a feature. Tepid middle = the worst.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum if logged-in app, v4-v5 for SaaS

scenario: User picks image, crops to required ratio

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: User can't preview crop, ratio not enforced, server-side compute wasted

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No zoom, no rotate, no flip, output is canvas not optimized

v3_senior.code:
// Use Canvas/OffscreenCanvas to compute cropped image at upload time
const canvas=new OffscreenCanvas(crop.width,crop.height);canvas.getContext('2d').drawImage(img,crop.x,crop.y,crop.width,crop.height,0,0,crop.width,crop.height);const blob=await canvas.convertToBlob({type:'image/webp',quality:0.85})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Real cropping not just display, WebP for size, OffscreenCanvas non-blocking

v4_staff.code:
// Use cropper libraries (Cropper.js, react-easy-crop) with zoom, rotation. Compose with image-compression.
// Output: cropped + resized + compressed before upload
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Full editor UX, pipeline of transforms, mobile-friendly gestures

v5_principal.code:
// Image editing is a workflow: pick, crop, adjust, preview, replace. State machine. Undo per step. Aspect can be free or constrained based on use case (avatar = 1:1, banner = 16:9).
// Treat as editor, not 'crop step'.
v5_principal.the_realization: 'Crop before upload' is the wedge for image editing: rotation, filters, redaction (blur faces). Avatar uploaders that do only crop are missing 80% of the value users want.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 with library, v5 for media-focused

scenario: E-commerce: hover image, see zoomed area

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Can't see detail

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Pixel low-res, lens jitter, mobile gets nothing

v3_senior.code: // High-res image lazy-loaded on hover. Lens shows portion of hires. ;
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Two-source (fast preview + hi-res on demand), real zoom

v4_staff.code:
// Tile-based zoom (deep-zoom / OpenSeadragon) for large images. Pinch-zoom on mobile.
// Best for high-MP product photos
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Scales to massive images, mobile-first gestures, used by museums/maps

v5_principal.code:
// Product imagery is conversion-critical. Multiple angles, video, 3D view, AR (Quick Look on iOS). Image zoom is one piece.
<model-viewer/>;// or USDZ for AR
v5_principal.the_realization: Product image is conversion. Zoom is one tool; 360, video, AR are others. E-commerce teams that ship 'hover to zoom' miss the 30% conversion lift from interactive media. Treat product visuals as a portfolio.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for serious e-com, v4 for premium, v5 strategic

feature_concept: Drag to upload + drag to reorder same component
scenario: List of uploaded files; user can drag external files to add, drag existing to reorder

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Drop conflict: dragging existing file might be interpreted as upload

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Drop indicator different per type, dnd-kit conflicts with raw drag events

v3_senior.code:
// dnd-kit for reorder + native drop for upload, on different layers
<DndContext>...<div onDragOver={isExternal} onDrop={uploadIfExternal}>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Two systems coexist, clear visual feedback per type

v4_staff.code:
// Unified picker: any source (external file, paste, click, drag-existing) routed through same flow
<MediaList accept={{external:upload,internal:reorder,paste:upload,camera:upload}}/>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Source-agnostic, consistent UX, accessible alternatives for each

v5_principal.code:
// Drag is one input vector among many. Design around 'add', 'reorder', 'delete' as actions. Inputs map to actions. Same action from different inputs = same result.
// Action-first, input-second.
v5_principal.the_realization: DnD complexity comes from input-first thinking. Action-first thinking ('add file to list', 'reorder list') simplifies: each input dispatches an action, components react to actions. Reordering by drag, keyboard, or context-menu all dispatch the same 'reorder' action.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious apps

scenario: User chooses which notifications to receive

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: All-or-nothing, users disable everything to silence one annoying type

v2_mid_level.code:
// Matrix: notification type × channel (in-app, email, push)
const prefs={comments:{inApp:true,email:false,push:false}}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Overwhelming UI, no smart defaults, no digest option, no quiet hours

v3_senior.code: // Grouped by importance: critical (account, security) always on; activity (comments, mentions) configurable; marketing opt-in. Sensible defaults.
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Importance hierarchy, smart defaults, can't disable critical, marketing opt-in (not opt-out)

v4_staff.code:
// Digest mode: 'email once daily summary'. Quiet hours. Frequency caps. Per-project (in Linear/GitHub style).
const pref={type:'comments',channel:'email',mode:'digest-daily',quietHours:['22:00','07:00']}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Digest reduces noise, quiet hours respect timezone, project-scoped

v5_principal.code:
// Notifications as relationship management. Track engagement (open/click rates). Auto-throttle on disengagement. Resurrection campaigns. Treat as CRM, not toggles.
// Privacy: GDPR consent, separate from product use.
v5_principal.the_realization: Notification prefs are the single biggest determinant of whether users stay or churn from annoyance. Designed well, they're customer-retention; designed poorly, they're 'unsubscribe from all'. The matrix UI is the easy part; the routing logic is everything.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for SaaS

scenario: Warn user before session expires due to inactivity

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: User loses unsaved work

v2_mid_level.code:
// Modal at 13 minutes ('logging out in 2 min')
useEffect(()=>{const t=setTimeout(()=>setShowWarning(true),13*60*1000);return()=>clearTimeout(t)},[])
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Activity not tracked (active user logged out), no extend option, no save before logout

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Activity-aware, warning at threshold, extend button

v4_staff.code:
// Server is source of truth for session. Frontend polls or uses SSE. Auto-save before timeout.
useEffect(()=>{const es=new EventSource('/session');es.onmessage=e=>{const {expiresIn}=JSON.parse(e.data);if(expiresIn<120)showWarning()}},[])
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Server-driven, multi-tab consistent, auto-save unsaved data

v5_principal.code:
// Session is a security boundary. Sensitive actions require re-auth even within session. Inactivity timeout is one signal; risk-based (new IP, suspicious behavior) is another. Step-up auth pattern.
// Save work without timing out: separate 'save' from 'session'.
v5_principal.the_realization: Session timeout is a security trade-off: too short annoys, too long risks abandoned sessions. Step-up auth (re-verify for sensitive ops) plus longer sessions is better than tight timeouts. Modern auth is risk-based, not duration-based.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for SaaS, v5 for security-critical

scenario: Users with vision needs require high contrast

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: WCAG violation, users blocked

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: One overlay rule doesn't fix component-specific contrast, doesn't help non-OS users

v3_senior.code:
// Explicit high-contrast theme with manual toggle + OS detection
:root[data-contrast='high']{--text:#fff;--bg:#000;--border:#fff;--focus:#ff0}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Coordinated theme, user-controllable, focus rings extra-visible

v4_staff.code:
// Forced-colors mode (Windows High Contrast)
@media (forced-colors:active){.btn{border:1px solid CanvasText;forced-color-adjust:none}}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Respects OS forced-colors (Windows users), uses system colors (CanvasText, ButtonFace)

v5_principal.code:
// Accessibility is multi-axis: contrast, motion, density, font size, color vision deficiency (CVD) modes. CVD simulators in dev, automated contrast checks in CI (axe, pa11y).
// Inclusive design: not 'extra mode' but 'all modes work well'.
v5_principal.the_realization: High contrast isn't a feature; it's a regulatory requirement in EU (EAA 2025) and increasingly elsewhere. Building it after the fact is expensive; tokens + CSS-first design make it cheap. Accessibility is product.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for compliance

feature_concept: Loading bar at top during navigation
scenario: YouTube-style red bar shows on slow page transitions

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Manual everywhere, no router integration

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Bar flashes on fast nav, no progress estimation

v3_senior.code:
// Debounce showing (delay 200ms) — fast nav no bar; slow nav shows
useEffect(()=>{if(nav.state==='loading'){const t=setTimeout(()=>setShow(true),200);return()=>clearTimeout(t)}else setShow(false)},[nav.state])
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: No flash for fast nav, progress estimate based on past navigation timings

v4_staff.code:
// useTransition for client-side state changes too
const [pending,startTransition]=useTransition();// Wrap nav-causing actions in startTransition
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Unified pending state across server nav and client transitions, React-native

v5_principal.code:
// Navigation UX is more than a bar. Optimistic UI for predictable nav (cache hit = instant). Skeleton for known routes. Real progress for upload-style nav.
// View Transitions API for shared-element animations during nav.
v5_principal.the_realization: Loading bars admit slowness. Optimistic UI eliminates the wait. View Transitions make change feel continuous. The loading bar is the last-resort UI; the better question is 'why is navigation slow?' (cache, prefetch, RSC).

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 React 19, v5 for polish

scenario: Failed request should retry, but not infinitely

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No backoff, retries on 4xx (waste), no max

v2_mid_level.code: for(let i=0;i<3;i++){try{return await fetch(url)}catch{await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,1000*(i+1)))}}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Retries 4xx (client errors won't fix themselves), no jitter, thundering herd

v3_senior.code:
// Exponential backoff with jitter, skip non-retryable (4xx)
for(let i=0;i<3;i++){try{const r=await fetch(url);if(r.status>=400&&r.status<500)return r;if(r.ok)return r}catch{}await new Promise(r=>setTimeout(r,Math.random()*1000*Math.pow(2,i)))}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Exponential + jitter, 4xx not retried, status-aware

v4_staff.code:
// React Query handles retry config + dedup + cache
useQuery({queryKey,queryFn,retry:(failureCount,error)=>error.status>=500&&failureCount<3,retryDelay:attempt=>Math.min(1000*2**attempt+Math.random()*500,30000)})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles complexity, declarative config, integrates with cache

v5_principal.code:
// Retries are part of distributed systems. Idempotency keys for mutations (avoid duplicate side effects on retry). Circuit breaker at API gateway. Retry budgets per user/session to prevent waste.
// Server-side retry-after header respected.
v5_principal.the_realization: Retries solve transient failures but create amplified ones (thundering herd, duplicate writes). Idempotency keys, circuit breakers, jitter, and budgets are the real fix. Naive retry loops are the source of most outages.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 use library, v5 for production

scenario: App uses more memory over time

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Only Chrome, manual, no aggregation

v2_mid_level.code: // Heap snapshots in DevTools — manually take and diff
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Manual, requires expertise, after-the-fact

v3_senior.code:
// Common leaks: forgotten event listeners, intervals, large closures in useEffect dependencies, unmounted components in observers
useEffect(()=>{const id=setInterval(...);return()=>clearInterval(id)},[])
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Disciplined cleanup, audit observers/intervals/listeners, weak refs where appropriate

v4_staff.code:
// performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory() (cross-origin isolation needed)
await performance.measureUserAgentSpecificMemory();// Returns total per-frame memory
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Production-grade memory measurement, alerts on growth

v5_principal.code:
// Memory is a system. SPAs with long sessions accumulate; ensure mount/unmount cleans. SSR/RSC eliminate the long-session problem for static content. Long-lived caches sized with eviction.
// LRU caches, WeakMap for component-instance data.
v5_principal.the_realization: Memory leaks in React come from observer/interval/listener neglect or closure-over-mutable-state. Modern React (cleanup discipline, hooks rules) mitigates; long-session SPAs still need vigilance. RSC reduces the surface.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for long-session apps
